title: Why Walking Is the Best Commute
passage: In my view, walking is the most underrated way to get to work. It costs nothing, needs no parking space, and gives you time to clear your head before the day begins. A twenty-minute walk can replace a stressful bus ride and a crowded elevator. Of course, walking is not practical for everyone, especially in bad weather or across long distances. Still, for people who live within a mile of their workplace, walking is often faster than driving once you count traffic and parking.

questions.question: What is the writer's main opinion?
options: Everyone should move closer to work
questions.explanation: The writer opens by saying walking is the most underrated way to get to work. The whole paragraph supports that opinion with reasons.

questions.question: Give one benefit of walking that the writer mentions.
questions.explanation: The writer lists several benefits, including that walking costs nothing, needs no parking, and helps clear your head. Any of these points would be correct.

questions.question: What limitation does the writer acknowledge?
options: Walking is not practical for everyone
Walking always takes more than an hour
questions.correct_answer: Walking is not practical for everyone
questions.explanation: The writer admits walking is not practical for everyone, especially in bad weather or over long distances. This shows a balanced view.

title: Schools Should Start Later
passage: I believe middle and high schools should start no earlier than eight thirty in the morning. Teenagers need more sleep than adults, yet many students wake up before six to catch an early bus. Research links later start times to better attendance, higher test scores, and fewer car accidents involving young drivers. Critics worry that later schedules will disrupt after-school jobs and sports. Those concerns are real, but they can be managed with flexible practice times and employer cooperation.

questions.question: What start time does the writer recommend?
questions.explanation: The writer argues that middle and high schools should start no earlier than eight thirty. That is the specific time proposed in the paragraph.

questions.question: Name one benefit of later start times mentioned in the text.
questions.explanation: Research linked to later start times shows better attendance, higher test scores, and fewer accidents. Any one of these outcomes is a valid answer.

questions.question: What concern do critics raise?
options: Schedules will disrupt jobs and sports
questions.correct_answer: Schedules will disrupt jobs and sports
questions.explanation: Critics worry that later schedules will disrupt after-school jobs and sports. The writer acknowledges this concern before offering solutions.

questions.question: How does the writer suggest managing critics' concerns?
questions.correct_answer: flexible practice times and employer cooperation
questions.explanation: The writer says concerns can be managed with flexible practice times and employer cooperation. These ideas aim to balance sleep needs with other activities.

title: Reading Books vs. Watching Videos
passage: Videos are convenient, but books train your attention in a way screens rarely do. When you read, you set the pace, pause to think, and imagine scenes in your own style. Video platforms reward quick reactions and constant scrolling, which can make deep focus harder later. I am not saying we should abandon video entirely; documentaries and lectures can inspire curiosity. However, if you want stronger vocabulary and calmer thinking, thirty minutes with a book often beats an hour of random clips.

questions.question: What advantage of reading does the writer emphasize?
questions.explanation: The writer says books train your attention in a way screens rarely do. That is the central advantage discussed in the paragraph.

questions.question: What can readers do that the writer values?
questions.explanation: When you read, you set the pace, pause to think, and imagine scenes yourself. The writer values this control and reflection.

questions.question: What does the writer say about video platforms?
options: They reward quick reactions and scrolling
They are better than books for vocabulary
questions.correct_answer: They reward quick reactions and scrolling
questions.explanation: The writer notes that video platforms reward quick reactions and constant scrolling. This habit can make deep focus harder later.

questions.question: What type of video content does the writer still praise?
questions.explanation: The writer is not against all video. Documentaries and lectures can inspire curiosity and still have value.

questions.question: According to the writer, what often beats an hour of random clips?
questions.explanation: The writer concludes that thirty minutes with a book often beats an hour of random clips if you want stronger vocabulary and calmer thinking.

title: Everyone Should Learn to Cook
passage: Cooking is a basic life skill, not a hobby reserved for experts. When you cook at home, you control ingredients, save money, and waste less food. Even simple meals—rice, eggs, soup—build confidence in the kitchen. Some people say they are too busy to cook, but many quick recipes take less time than waiting for delivery. Schools and community centers should offer short, practical classes so cooking feels approachable, not intimidating.

questions.question: How does the writer describe cooking?
questions.explanation: The writer opens by calling cooking a basic life skill, not something only experts should do. That framing supports the main argument.

questions.question: Give one reason the writer gives for cooking at home.
questions.explanation: Cooking at home lets you control ingredients, save money, and waste less food. Any of these reasons answers the question well.

questions.question: What does the writer suggest schools and community centers should offer?
questions.explanation: The writer recommends short, practical classes so cooking feels approachable. The goal is to make the skill less intimidating.

title: Cities Need More Parks
passage: Urban parks are not a luxury; they are essential public space. Trees cool streets, clean the air, and give families a free place to exercise and socialize. When cities sell parkland for development, they trade long-term health for short-term profit. I support building housing, but not at the expense of every green corner. A city with accessible parks is a city where children can play safely and adults can recover from stress without spending money.

questions.question: What is the writer's view of urban parks?
options: They should be replaced by shops
questions.explanation: The writer states clearly that urban parks are not a luxury but essential public space. This is the main claim of the paragraph.

questions.question: Name one environmental benefit of trees mentioned in the text.
questions.explanation: The writer says trees cool streets and clean the air. Either benefit would be correct, as both are named in the passage.

questions.question: What does the writer criticize?
questions.explanation: The writer criticizes cities that sell parkland for development. They believe this trades long-term health for short-term profit.

questions.question: Who benefits from accessible parks, according to the writer?
questions.explanation: The writer says children can play safely and adults can recover from stress in parks. Accessible green space helps people of all ages.
